notes.note: Cicero quotes the Greek 'gelōta sardanion,' a bitter, forced laugh (proverbially the rictus of those who ate a poisonous Sardinian plant). The pun also glances at Tigellius the Sardinian from the previous letter.
'Manum de tabula!' — a proverbial cry ('hands off the picture/slate!') warning an artist or pupil to stop before spoiling the work; here a mock schoolroom command to break off their literary play.

notes.anchor: The schoolmaster is on us sooner than we had thought
notes.note: Cicero keeps up the schoolboy conceit: he and Gallus are pupils caught at their writing-tablets, and the 'schoolmaster' (perhaps Caesar, or critical opinion) has arrived to punish their amateur 'Cato' pamphlets.

A pun on 'in catomum' (a schoolboy flogging given while hoisted on another's shoulders) and 'Catonianos' (the writers of pamphlets praising Cato of Utica): the would-be eulogists risk a schoolmaster's beating.

notes.anchor: the pen is the workman of speech
notes.note: 'Stilus est dicendi opifex': Cicero's maxim that practised writing (with the stilus, the wax-tablet pen) is the true craftsman of eloquence, urging Gallus not to stray a finger's breadth from it.
